content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for age-appropriate, non-animated movies to watch with 7 year old nephew

**Score:** 32
**Body:** I (52F) spend a lot of time with my 7 year-old nephew. Lately he and I have had a lot of fun picking movies to watch together.

He prefers movies with “real people” versus animated films and he seems to really like movies with animals or a child lead/main character. It’s hard to find age-appropriate movies that aren’t animated. Some of the G rated movies we’ve tried he said were “for babies”. 😉

Some films we have watched together that he has really enjoyed so far are:

•Dog movies - Dog, A Dog’s Journey, A Dog’s Purpose, A Dog’s Way Home, Dog Gone, Hachi A Dog’s Tale, Benji, Rescued by Ruby, The Healing Powers of Dude

•Horse movies - Seabiscuit and Secretariat

•He’s really into dinosaurs right now so he’s seen every Jurassic movie multiple times and both Night at the Museum films. (He also LOVED the new documentary series The Dinosaurs on Netflix that came out last month.)

•Two Jennifer Garner family movies on Netflix - Yes Day and Family Switch

•And a few oldies - E.T., Honey I Shrunk the Kids, Wonder, Matilda, Home Alone 1&2, The Karate Kid, Rocky IV

He also enjoyed The Martian and Ford v. Ferrari.

His not wanting to watch animated movies has begun to make it difficult to find stuff he will enjoy that is age-appropriate, so I’m looking for some ideas for things we can watch together.

Looking for modern films and not stuff that is too dated like The Wizard of Oz. He hated that movie.

Thanks for your suggestions!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Second hand lions

Great movie that I think both of you will enjoy
2. Sandlot and Home Alone!
3. Babe 
4. Jumanji 
5. The Princess Bride 
6. If he is good with slightly older movies there is a slew of great options from the 80's & 90's

* Goonies
* Mighty Ducks
* Sandlot
* Never Ending Story
* and so on...

7 might be too young but he might vibe with some safer PG-13 type movies, I'm think something like Rocky or Back to the Future. My kids love the movie The Greatest Showman, but it is a musical & they are girls, so your milage might vary 😄
7. What about Star Wars? 

That came out when I was 5, and I saw it in the theaters and was blown away. I know the visuals/effects are very common these days, but maybe the story will draw him in? Just an idea, I didnt see it listed in your OP.
8. Dennis the Menace

Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles

Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles 2: Secret of the Ooze

The Explorers 

Flight of the Navigator

Milo and Otis

Babe

Homeward Bound
9. holes
10. The Black Stallion (1979)

## 3. Horror films from Spain

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I'm looking for recs of horror films made in Spain. So far, I have seen and enjoyed:

The Platform

Tin and Tina

Piggy

The Coffee Table

The Devil's Backbone

Anything else worth seeing? Thank you in advance

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. \[REC\]
  - Good rec 
2. The Orphanage (2007) is pretty good!
  - If by “pretty good” you mean “absolutely terrifying”, I agree!  One of the very few films I seriously considered just stopping because I was getting so creeped out - perhaps because also one of the few (if not the only) film where it’s completely understandable why someone would stay put instead of fleeing screaming into the night.
3. Does Pan’s Labyrinth count?
4. Timecrimes

Sooo good!

## 4. Movies to make you appreciate living

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I watched The Curious Case of Benjamin Button and it hit all the feels in the right place. Also really enjoyed The Intouchables. Going through a bit of a depressive phase and wanting to appreciate the beauty of life. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty.
2. Big Fish (2003)
3. about time. such a great moviee..
4. Pixar's Soul
  - This is the one.
5. Wings of Desire

Perfect Days
6. Train Dreams
7. Chocolat (2000) Johnny Depp & Juliette Binoche
Filmed in a quaint little French town. Definitely one which will lift your spirits!
8. in no particular order:  
Life is Beautiful , Pursuit of Happinness, The Secret Life of Walter Smitty, Lost in Translation, Being John Malkovich, The Truman Show
9. 'Me, Earl and the dying girl'.

## 5. Movies set between late 50s and early 70s in the US

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Preferably a movie accurate to the culture and aesthetic of the time. If you know one which fits the bill but doesnt take place in the US that is also fine.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. American Graffiti

Also for those saying George couldn’t write human dialogue, this proves otherwise.
2. The Sandlot


Stand By Me
3. Any of Alfred Hitchcock's movies from that era 
4. The Graduate
5. Rebel Without A Cause. 
6. The 2nd Godfather movie, Pleasantville, Good Morning Vietnam, and there are movies that pass through the time prominently, like Forrest Gump.
  - All good suggestions.

Totally forgot about Good Morning Vietnam and Pleasantville  
7. Easy Rider
8. Stand By Me (1986).  Set in 1959.

Thriteen Days (2000).  Set during the Cuban Missle Crisis in October 1962.
9. The founder. Ford v Ferrari. Man from uncle.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Joaquin Phoenix in Gladiator is one of the most malicious, twisted movie villains ever

**Score:** 2073
**Body:** Rewatched Gladiator recently and my hatred for Commudus hasn’t changed one bit.

This has to be up there with one of the most nefarious movie villains ever. Every single act is so wicked, you literally cheer in the final fight!

Not to mention a coward as well. Joaquin Phoenix absolutely crushed it in the role.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes he crushed it.   
A good villain makes a difference, look what the actor for Joffrey did  for game of thrones.
  - For reals!

I know everyone loves Crowe in this movie and deservedly so.

But what Phoenix does here is phenomenal. The “AM I NOT MERCIFUL?!?” moment always gives me chills. 
    - "Busy little bee" when Cassian(?, the nephew) is sitting with him and it's like the kid slowly gets what's up...damn.
  - Jack Gleeson killed it as Joffrey,  everytime that turd was on screen my blood pressure rose, I wish he died slower
2. Won’t argue against it. 

Jason Issac’s from The Patriot automatically comes to mind when I think of Villains. Not a movie but Collin Farrell from the Penguin series also comes to mind. He shows signs of being somewhat decent and just totally flips it. He’s vile and I love it. 
  - Jason Isaac’s in the Patriot is on a whole other level. 

Definitely top 10 worthy. 
3. Ramsey Bolton takes is for me 
4. He vexes me
  - I'm terribly vexed.
5. "They tell me your son squealed like a little girl when they nailed him to the cross. And your wife moaned like a whore as they ravaged her again, and again."

I honestly had trouble enjoying his scenes in Signs when it came out because I couldn't get over his character from Gladiator 

## 2. Any fans of "Face/Off" (1997)? It's one of my favourite 90's action movies.

**Score:** 389
**Body:** Sean Archer and Castor Troy are like Batman and Joker for me. Both are very entertaining characters to watch them fighting each other. I own every platform of this movie on DVD, VHS, Blu Ray and 4K. I watch it at least 2 times a year. My mom introduced me to this movie. When this movie came out I wasn't born yet. I watched it for the first time when I was around 15 years old. I'm not a big fan of John Woo. I like a few of his works but I really enjoy Face/Off. I think this is his best work in Hollywood. He prefers his Hong Kong films but Face/Off is so special for me when I watch because the story is crazy about F.B.I. agent have to surgery rip off his face to become the villain and goes to deep cover in the jail. Both Nic Cage and Travolta were awesome in this movie especially Nic Cage. Sometimes his performance is over the top and so laughable but I love this guy. It is so rare to see both lead actors play good and bad in the same movie. It got every John Woo trademark. The slow motion in the gunfight and doves are flying everywhere. I like the final boat chase scene. I think it's one of the best boat chase scenes I have ever seen. It was so ridiculous but awesome at the same time. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|3YGKFfw611fZS|downsized)
  - ![gif](giphy|WLQ3qRdHXAMlW)
2. I’m still struck by how John Travolta played Nic Cage better than Nic Cage, a feat rivaled only by Danny Pudi.
  - Always Be Caging.
3. ![gif](giphy|hwZ51FKy98qv6)
4. ![gif](giphy|L33OnjCuZ5b13v4Vy1)
5. Is it cheesy and over the top? Yes. Is it awesome because its cheesy and over the top? Yes. 
  - But that’s what makes it a Nicholas Cage movie. lol
6. This and ConAir! Just 90‘s peak! I loved em both. So entertaining, and somehow funny😂
  - Don't forget The Rock!

## 4. Roland Emmerich’s 2012 made me remember why I love disaster movies

**Score:** 315
**Body:** Was randomly watching TV and 2012 came on. I told myself I’d watch 5 mins & go back to work, next thing I know I’m fully locked in with snacks like I chose this. 

On paper this movie really shouldn’t work that well. The CGI is hit or miss, the logic is barely holding together and everything is pushed to the extreme. Usually I’m the kind of person who notices all that and it ruins the movie for me.But with something like this, that instinct just fades into the background. It’s like the movie sets its own rules early like.. bro this is going to be chaos and if you accept that, everything else falls into place.

I think thats what disaster movies rely on more than anything. Its the commitment to spectacle. Not just having big moments, but constantly escalating them. 2012 does that really well. It doesn’t pause too long, it doesn’t over explain, it just keeps moving from one massive set piece to another. Cities collapse, the ground splits open, oceans swallow everything, ships tilt like they weigh nothing & somehow there are giraffes being airlifted in the middle of all this. It’s excessive, sometimes ridiculous but never boring.

The story and acting is fine, mostly functional but that almost feels intentional.They exist to hold the viewer just enough so the spectacle doesn’t feel completely weightless.

It also made me think about how we judge these movies. Disaster films rarely aim for tight realism or deep character studies. They’re built around awe, scale, and momentum. When they succeed, it’s not because they’re believable, it’s because they are engaging despite not being believable.
I guess this is the whole point of the genre. 

Also..the dog survives, which I feel is important to mention. 7/10. Good movie. Recommend me your favorite, I'll add it to my watchlist. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Disaster movies are such dumb fun. 
  - Especially when you got someone like Woody playing in a role like this. Really sells it lol
  - Independence Day wasn’t dumb, it was fantastic. And yes it’s a disaster film. World gets trashed 
2. I enjoy this movie every time. I´m a sucker for disaster movies.
3. The summer of '97 had both Dantes Peak + Volcano w Tommy Lee Jones. A year after that, Armageddon + Deep Impact. Late nineties was peak disaster movie era
  - Volcano. I'll never forget it's tagline "the coast is toast" 
4. Disaster Movies are my Guilty Pleasure Genre, more or less, with no real Guilt.


2012, Armageddon, The Core, and so on, I find them all quite entertaining.


The more fantastical with a stacked cast the better, take it seriously, for the lulz.
5. You want a pickle?
6. I was expecting as he fell, Yuri would have said in slow-motion: “Re-mem-ber, I, was, Rrrruuussssiiiiaaaannnnnn!!!”
7. Totally mindless entertainment. No thinking needed. Just sit back and eat your candy, and popcorn.

## 5. Seeing Black Hawk Down in a movie theater when it was released in 2001 was an awesome experience. Epic movie with great acting and action. Although it was criticized for inaccuracies it was still such a great movie. When you heard, “ Super six one is hit he is hit” man goosebumps all over. Thoughts?

**Score:** 132

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's really not that inaccurate in what it actually shows, it just leaves out a bunch of context which somewhat whitewashes the conflict and minimises the role the UN played in the rescue.

Otherwise, very well made. 
  - 10th Mtn Div and UN forces fought hard too.  Pretty much left out of the story…still a good movie 
2. It’s the best modern military film to date. With an all star cast. Nothing comes close to capturing what that film did. 

It also helped that Scott had a great relationship with the US military and could utilize their assets to bring the film credibility to S tier and raise the bar to such a high level 
  - Warfare is great and has a similar feel.  I prefer Black Hawk Down, but I did see it in a theater vs at home for Warfare.
    - I feel BHD was a better production and a better telling of a story. Warfare was closer to a reenactment of what could have been a collection of go pro video. Very accurate, but not necessarily a better movie. 
      - Agree, Warfare was exceptional - but the narrative was very episodelike.

Black Hawk Down is so much more Dramatic.

Both very intense movies.
3. Loved the Mike Durant interrogation scene. “None of you Americans smoke anymore. You live long, dull, and uninteresting lives” I also really liked the soundtrack
4. Its no Shaggy D.A. thats for sure

https://preview.redd.it/qravthlbx9yg1.jpeg?width=295&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=6c8925b279871a239ecfa1788a30e5ee9be4c85a
5. https://i.redd.it/ss41p8qv4ayg1.gif
6. My biggest problem with Black Hawk Down. Is that it mentions the 19 dead US servicemen. But not the UN Malaysian driver that was killed rescuing the Americans. Like WTF did he ever do to not get a mention or any respect!

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. A Review of 'Hudson Hawk' (1991)

**Score:** 31
**Body:** ***'How am I driving? 1-800-I'm-gonna-fuckin'-die!'***

'Hudson Hawk' is berserk. Madcap. A visual synonym for 'rambunctious'. It does not even try to be connected to reality at any point. It is often described as a live-action cartoon, and that is as close as you are going to get for descriptors. For god knows what reason, Sony unveiled an associated video game not long after the film bombed at the box office. That did not go well, of course. I genuinely cannot understand how they greenlit a video game for this—I cannot even understand why they agreed to spend $65 million big ones on the movie itself. But, boy oh boy, am I glad they did.

Bruce Willis clearly has a demented sense of humour; he received his sole writing credit on this production. Every zany line he throws out is like watching him subsume Brad Pitt's character from '12 Monkeys', which Willis starred in. The main problem with that notion is that 'Hudson Hawk' was released four years prior to that film. I started to wonder whether Willis was just taking the mickey as he went along with it all, because not only did he and the producers initially promote Hawk's escapades as a 'Die Hard-esque action blockbuster', but every line said in the movie is ironic on some level. It is quite the achievement when you have serious money to recoup. After watching it, I was fantasising about how the inclusion of famously unhinged actor Nicolas Cage would cut like butter for a romp like this.

Willis's Hawk, a cat burglar released from a decade of imprisonment, is joined by his long-term crime partner, Tommy Five-Tone, who is played by Danny Aiello. Willis's unmoored performance is without a doubt enjoyable to watch; he is hilarious with line delivery, his natural face carries an ideal, permanent split of half-confused/half-reckless, and he is having fun. Aiello, however, is once again the ballast in a production. His screen time is somewhat limited in the middle, but his presence is always yearned for; he has all of the comic qualities of a fun sidekick and partner in some proportion: faithful (in the end), capable (to a degree), and present (when you need him). I have a real affinity for Danny Aiello. The pairing uses millisecond-perfect songs to time their burglaries, so there exists a whole host of Aiello/Willis karaoke recordings inserted into the multiple scenes of theft. That musical element is the cherry on top of the story's jam-packed cake of chaos.

The film also begins with a ludicrous, almost self-serious spoof of Leonardo da Vinci at work, somehow converting lead to gold via a very literal version of 'deus ex machina'. This soon transitions into modern day, with Hawk prison sentence coming to an end. One thing you will notice as a running gag in the film are the inexplicable transitions from one scene to another. It happened a few times and had me rewinding. Another motif is the impossibility for Hawk to find some quiet and an unspoiled cup of cappuccino. It is in the not-so-lofty dreams and desires of Hawk, like that cappuccino, that the film finds its heart amidst a background of noise.

The remainder of the cast is occupied by names: you have Richard E. Grant stealing scenes with, going back to the adjective, cartoon villainy and even bigger acting. He plays one half of the villainous couple in the film, the British Darwin Mayflower; 'Darwin' is no doubt a misnomer, for the character is an avaricious, inane, aristocratic knave who serves up endlessly quotable lines such as 'Tommy, you New-York-Italian-father-made-twenty-bucks-a-week son-of-a-bitch' and 'I'll kill your friends, your family, and the bitch you took to the prom!'

His other half, Minerva, portrayed by Sandra Bernhard, is just as misnamed. Minerva has the foremost line of dialogue, 'Bunny, Ball Ball!'. That is one of the more barmy dog commands I have heard and ends up being the downfall of the dog. The couple heads Mayflower Industries and seeks to… Run the world, of course. And metamorphose lead to gold, like da Vinci 'did'. Bernhard, like Grant, turns in a supremely BIG performance, and that, at least, is worth its weight in gold by the end.

Another important villain is James Coburn, who plays CIA figurehead George Kaplan. Kaplan is in league with the Mayflower two and seeks the same as them. Kaplan brings with him a selection of chocolate. Well, his agents are all codenamed after chocolates. All of the chocolates are personalised with riveting quirks and behave so that complete suspension of disbelief, above-and-beyond the already mentioned, is required. The Mario Brothers of New Jersey (a nod to Nintendo and also built-in video game promotion) are played by Frank Stallone and Carmine Zozzoro. The casting of Frank is subversion in and of itself. Hawk is forced by the Mario brothers to burgle a museum for da Vinci's model Sforza horse, and then later he is transported to Rome by force to continue thieving for them until the syndicate compiles the components for their lead-to-gold machine.

Andie MacDowell's Anna Bargali, a sort of hesitant nun at the Vatican, is the heroine and Hawk's love interest. MacDowell plays her with a constant sense of conflict and craftiness. The romance between Hawk and Bargali is fundamentally unbelievable, but we are made to root for them as the escapades progress, and they do work as a pairing. The trio they end up forming with Tommy included is as endearing as any two-criminals-and-a-nun triumvirate. MacDowell's drug-addled dolphin sounds, 'I must speak with the dolphins now…' Eeeee-eeee-eeee-eeeeee!' is quite the sound for sore ears.

The 1990s was a decade replete with cinematic masterworks. 'Hudson Hawk'… Is probably not one of them. But it is necessary levity, a concoction of acid-trip proportions. I enjoyed watching this far more than I thought I would, from the Hawk/Tommy loft apartment hideout in New Jersey to their first on-screen burglary to the anarchy that permeates every second they spend in Rome, scored by a coterie of miscreants. This film has achieved cult-classic status, I think, and if it has not, then I will do my part to ensure it does. Sometimes the unserious deserve to be taken more seriously. How many other films feature a car chase where the main character somehow drives a gurney?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Truly a vanity project of insane proportions, and I still can't help but get caught up in the ride. As I've said about this film before, letting Bruce Willis not only improv his own jokes but sing *multiple times* in the film was like shooting yourself in both feet, only to find out you can fly.
  - Don't forget Aiello's singing! Funnily enough, both of them have released music albums. 
    - I could never forget Aiello's singing 🥹
      - Thank gawd. 
2. I saw this in the dollar theater not long before it hit video, and trust me when I say that I was on an island in my enjoyment of this movie. The Swinging on a Star synchronization absolutely hooked me.

I think that context is key for looking backwards at a movie - world's best example is always Citizen Kane. If you don't know anything about film and watch it through the lens of current movies, it's all things you've seen, seen, seen. If you watch it with context it's an innovative masterclass.

Willis was still coming off of Moonlighting - by the time that show ended its ratings were (comparatively) in the tank. The humor isn't miles away from Moonlighting, and by the time Hudson Hawk came out Willis was pretty well associated with Die Hard, overshadowing Moonlighting. He had giant draw, year over year - Die Hard, Look Who's Talking, Die Hard 2. Last Boy Scout came out the same year as Hudson Hawk. It's not like those were the only things he was in from 1988-1991, but that's absolutely the expectation we had of him. He was bankable, so the budget thrown at the movie made sense by that metric. Quirky comedy, he's carrying these by money grossers, he can carry Hudson Hawk. But by the time Hudson Hawk came out his public image was *not* in the same headspace as Moonlighting. We were in this tremendous cultural lull and poised for a giant shift. Garth Brooks released his first album the year prior, and Nirvana released Nevermind in September of that year. Both should give you a *lot* of insight about the state of things.
3.  I really enjoyed this review because I feel Hudson Hawk is one of those rare films that got way too much scorn on initial release and way too much praise on it's reevaluation. Realistically it's not a great film, but when taken lightly and watched with fairly low expectations its a goofy, fun movie which is fine. 

 You tread the line nicely between recommending a fun film you like without going overboard and trying to make it into a hidden masterpiece from an earlier age (which it isn't)
4. I watched this movie with my Grandfather, and we had a lot of fun with it. Core memory for me. 😊

I still remember Bruce Willis talking about it in interviews and saying how they intentionally wanted to make a movie for the fans and not for the critics. They were not at all bothered by the critical reaction.
5. “Bunny, Ball-ball...” Hudson Hawk in my Top 25 of all time. Hudson Hawk is a stylish, silly, misunderstood gem cult classic, that zips by in 100 minutes.

Siskel & Ebert famously didn't "get it" in their review either. Believe it or not but it takes a certain high brow intellectual understanding to appreciate the absurdist subtle humor of this film that lurks behind the wisecracks. It's like a combination of A Fish Called Wanda and Dumb & Dumber.

There are lots of social critiques in Hudson Hawk that fly right over peoples' heads. Turning lead into gold and crashing the world monetary system is right up there with the briefcase full of IOUs in Dumb & Dumber, and as pertinent as ever with world fiat paper currencies on the verge of hyperinflationary collapse. The bar being yuppified with Captain Bob's steering wheel relocated to the back office. “Reindeer goat cheese pizza!” The Vatican involvement. Leonardo da Vinci mystery. The ex-CIA goons with candybar code names (Butterfinger, played by Andrew Bryniarski “Keep laughing, Jumbo --- It’s (soda) coming out my nose”, “Read my lips: steak burger!”) that were upgraded from venereal diseases. The location sets. The feel good romantic vibes.

The slapstick cracks are some of the best hilariously quotable off guard quips in league with the best of the best spoof and mockumentary films (Airplane, Spinal Tap, Best In Show). Singing songs being used to time robberies is very original. The film is a smart sassy mood vibe with high brow elements that absolutely *whooooosh* the ever living fuck out of the uninitiated that don't pay close attention or pick up details through repeat viewings. “Hey, this doesn’t taste like cappuccino. Oh, I guess I put too much ethyl chloride in it”. That’s its real sin. It’s smarter than the critics. How dare a troglodyte action star like Bruce Willis dunk on them with such a writing credit wit! Not just from the main characters but from all the great supporting characters. Richard E. Grant and Sandra Bernhard (“This is supposed to be torture, not therapy”) grand theft auto heist their scenes!

## 2. Casual Discussion Thread (April 30, 2026)

## 3. Foreign Film Month Challenge

**Score:** 5
**Body:** I made it a goal for the month of April to watch 10 foreign language (non-English) movies. Today, I accomplished that goal.

Ranking of films watched:

1.	⁠Incendies  
2.	⁠La Haine  
3.	⁠Anatomy of a Fall  
4.	⁠Life is Beautiful  
5.	⁠in the Mood for Love  
6.	⁠Come and See  
7.	⁠City of God  
8.	⁠Memories of Murder  
9.	⁠Another Round  
10.	⁠High and Low

The experience was very enjoyable! I had a lot of fun researching foreign films that were critically acclaimed or that I might be interested in. I watched the first 10 movies listed here. I ordered the movies based on my level of enjoyment. The French language films I watched (Incendies, La Haine and Anatomy of a Fall) all completely blew me away!

Overall, I was very happy with the movies I chose to watch from my list, ranking all of the films at least 4 stars or above (with the exception of Another Round and High and Low). My biggest disappointment was not connecting with High and Low. The movie was well done but, I was not engaged and interested in the dialogue and emotion like I had hoped. Although I wasn’t foreign-language averse before, I definitely have a deeper appreciation and interest in foreign films now. I am excited to continue to watch more films from here.

What are your favorite non-English films? Any rankings from my list that surprise you? Do you think watching foreign language films separates casual movie watchers from cinephiles?

Link to my full Foreign Film Month Watchlist below

## 4. The Problem with Music Biopics

**Score:** 4
**Body:** In light of the new Michael Jackson biopic, I wrote down some thoughts on the genre of musical biopics as a whole. Although, admittedly I haven’t  watched *Michael* (and I’m not sure I will), so this analysis ignores it altogether. So heads up, I’m sorry if that’s annoying.

Anyway, I recently watched “Control”, the 2007 biopic about Joy Division singer Ian Curtis, directed by Anton Corbijn. Now, I went in to the film with pretty high expectations, as far as music biopics go, this one seems to be among the more respected ones. From what I can see it received great reviews across the board, most of the reviews on letterboxd are very positive, and it even premiered at and won awards at Cannes. But I have to say, I was very disappointed. I found the film to be mostly dead, unimaginative, shallow, and while I wouldn’t say Sam Riley did a bad job, I also just didn’t really believe I was watching Ian Curtis. And that is at the heart of my argument, and the central point of why I think music biopics, not just Control, have a presence problem that just cannot be resolved. Mark Fisher puts it well on his essay on Joy Division from “Ghosts of My Life”

“Rock depends crucially on a particular body and a particular voice and the mysterious relationship between the two. *Control* could never make good the loss of Ian Curtis’s voice and body, and so ended up as arthouse karaoke naturalism; the actors could simulate the chords, could ape Curtis’s moves, but they couldn’t forge the vortical charisma, couldn’t muster the unwitting necromantic art that transformed the simple musical structures into a ferocious expressionism, a portal to the outside”  

*Karaoke naturalism* hits the nail on the head. During the live performance scenes in *Control*, Sam Riley does a good job, the eye-rolling, the voice, the spastic movement, are in fairness, all pretty close. But there is just something missing there. I just didn’t believe that was actually Ian Curtis, making the whole thing fall flat. After the film I found myself watching Joy Division performances on youtube, and the difference was immediately clear. The strange, dark, hypnotic power of both Curtis and the band as a whole, the “necromantic art” as Fisher puts it, that is so fascinating, just *wasn’t there.* 

This is exactly what most music biopic performances end up feeling like, they’re karaoke, they’re impressions, albeit often commendable ones, but rarely do they feel like the real thing. This is a huge problem for the genre,  the reason these rock and pop gods became iconic enough to warrant biopics in the first place is precisely because of that presence that the films fail to replicate. This is why they often feel so pointless, they fail to do the one thing that justifies their existence. These films often feel like overly-produced impressions that are trying desperately to try and fool you into thinking they’re the real thing, but how often do they actually manage it?

Among the more well-regarded biopics in recent years we have *Rocketman, Straight Outta Compton,* and *Love & Mercy*. These seem to me to be the more frequently cited examples of the genre done right. However, I have to say that while I mostly agree that they’re among the better ones, these three films are still mostly just *fine*. I don’t think they could be considered great films by any means, so is that the highest the genre can reach for? Fine? 

As to why those three succeed, I think *Rocketman* is inventive enough and fun enough with how it stages its musical numbers for it to be a thoroughly entertaining viewing experience, and *Straight Outta Compton* and *Love & Mercy* manage to be fairly interesting, well-structured dramas, which is more than we can say for the tedious, paint-by-the-numbers narratives we tend to get from this genre. *Love & Mercy* in particular has something interesting going on with its double timeline and double casting of the central role of Brian Wilson. In their own ways these films are actually trying cinematic ideas outside of just pointing at their star and saying “Aren’t they great? Aren’t they special?” over and over again. 

These three films also feature decent, embodied performances that actually do manage to channel the artists somewhat, or at the very least manage to hold their ground as good and interesting performances in their own right. This is particularly the case with *Love and Mercy*, both Paul Dano and John Cusack arrive at genuinely interesting performances that allow you to get lost in the film, that don’t constantly remind you that you’re watching an imitation of something else (I’m looking at you, Austin Butler). 

An advantage these three films all have, I think, is that their subjects also aren’t particularly known for their live performances. These artists are more well known for their studio catalogue than their physical presence (at least not like Elvis, Michael Jackson, or even the comparatively underground Ian Curtis). These are artist that we know predominately through their voices. To go back to Mark Fisher, these are artists for whom the *body* factor holds significantly less wait in the equation. I imagine this allows the actors and filmmakers much more freedom to play, they can create robust characters and well-rounded performances that can take a life of their own, without constantly having to resort to Vegas-style impressions to try and impress the fans with their mimicry.

Another recent example that I’ve heard is decent is *Better Man,* but I haven’t seen it. Although I have to say that it at least tried something radically different by ditching the imitation angle altogether by having an ape as Robbie Williams. That sounds like an interesting and smart way to get around this problem. But it bombed, so studios will probably not want to try anything like that again anytime soon.

And that’s really the centre of it, as cinematically barren as this genre tends to be, it also almost always guarantees box office success, *Michael* being the biggest hit yet, so they’ll keep making ‘em for now. Although I wouldn’t be surprised if the upcoming *Beatles* films are the climactic end of the music biopic craze that has been going strong for around twenty years now. 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There are, in my mind, two main structural pillars that are problematic for any biopic/documentary but which seem particularly acute in musical films (or it could just be that there are so many of those). The first is authorized vs. unauthorized, and the second is what part of that person's life you show.

Authorized biopics have to be approved by the subject/family/estate, which means they tend to be glorifying and often downplay, ignore, or flat-out lie about negative aspects of the person's life. Bohemian Rhapsody, Michael, Walk The Line, and The Doors come to mind very particularly. The benefit is access to the music, writings, and other personal items that might otherwise not be usable. Alternately, an unauthorized biopic/documentary, like Nothing Compares about Sinéad O'Connor or the banned-by-the-estate docuseries about Prince, can tell whatever stories it wants but can only use publicly-available clips etc.. Important to point out that every filmmaker has an agenda and a slant, so unauthorized doesn't inherently mean it's more true to life, but authorized ones almost never tell even close to the full story. There were two movies made about Nina Simone about ten years ago: the authorized 'What Happened, Miss Simone?' documentary and the unauthorized biopic 'Nina'. Both were atrocious, for completely different reasons.

The second problem is that almost no human life can be condensed down to a two-hour story. If you're doing a Miles Davis, or Johnny Cash, or Billie Holiday biopic you couldn't possibly hope to to justice to even a single phase of their legacy in one film. Could you imagine even *trying* to wedge Prince's life and career into two hours?? So you either just combine multiple real people into a single fictional character and blur a ton of actual events into generalized scenarios intended to be representative (e.g. The Doors movie, Walk The Line, Bird, Lady Sings The Blues) or you just take a very specific slice of their life/career and strip away most of the context (e.g. What's Love Got To Do With It or Springsteen: Deliver Me from Nowhere). Either way you can't really be said to have explored or delivered the story of that person in any meaningful way.

Ultimately, and I think Michael's success is a great example of this, people seem to just want a glorified music concert of an artist that shows them things that couldn't be filmed at an actual concert for logistic reasons, and maybe a bit that makes them think they've "seen into the soul of the artist". They don't give a shit about fidelity to any of the artists' actual stories.
  - > They don’t give a shit about fidelity to any of the artists’ actual stories. 
  
Tell that to the asshole that was googling every single thing in _Michael_ by my side while I was trying to watch it.
2. A friend told me that I had to see "Bohemian Rhapsody," especially for the emotion of the Live Aid segment. I refused to watch it, having grown up with Queen's music and seeing Live Aid at the time of its broadcast. So for me, any musician  who I admire and came of age with, is off limits as a biopic. Give me concert videos and documentaries. Why would I want to see some clown parading around as a famed artist of my generation? I don't need the dramatization or the controversy they might drum up.

I'm more open to biopics of musicians whose heyday was before I was born. Again, if there are good documentaries or existing live footage, I might be on the fence. For example, I enjoyed "Bird" directed by Clint Eastwood and "Lady Sings the Blues" with Diana Ross. Or even the Ray Charles film with Jamie Foxx. But what do I know. Maybe those who saw those artists first-hand would feel differently. 
  - I agree with that. I know Cadillac records has a ton of issues but it did open me up to guys like howlin wolf who I had never heard of when I saw it. Even the temptations movie brought a lot of new artists to me.
3. Have you ever felt you saw the real Thor? Sorry, I don't want to spoil that for you. Real presidents in films with presidents? As with any movie, and biopics are movies, it needs some suspension of disbelief. 
  - Luckily for Marvel, there is no real Thor. So there is no one they are failing to recreate by depicting him. I made this post about music biopics specifically, and not just biopics in general, because musicians inherently have a physical presence which is a fundamental part of what fans are drawn to and what makes studios want to make those films, a presence that they then fail to meaningfully bring to screen. That’s why most of the films in the genre feel weird, flat, and empty. I’d go as far as to say it’s a mostly failed genre (cinematically speaking) and it’s interesting to look at why that is.
    - Of course, if the actual musician didn't play himself in the movie, it won't work for you. You could just say "i don't like music biopics" and end your review there. 

## 5. Thoughts on Andrzej Żuławski’s “The Devil”?

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I watched it for the first time this afternoon as part of a watchlist/film challenge I’ve created for myself. I should note that this was my first Żuławski film so I may not be familiar with his style. Even then thought it was pretty good, if quite a bit more disturbing and disorienting than I’m used to. The performances in particular were captivating and I liked the atmosphere of the film.

Unfortunately, the only way I could find this at all was via watching it on YouTube; someone uploaded what I believe to be the 2007 DVD transfer of the film. I suspect I would have better been able to appreciate the film had I had access to a higher-quality version. Unfortunately there’s no American blu-ray release and I’m unwilling to import a release from the UK and purchase a region-free player along with it. Hopefully someday a release is done in the states.

In any case, have any of you seen this film? And if so, what are your thoughts and opinions on it?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is one of my favorite films, and rewatching it again brought back many fond memories.

The frenetic handheld camerawork to achieve the fever-dream feeling is a common element in his work, although it varies in effect from sci-fi docu-style to comedy to surreality, depending on the film.

The use in this film is almost the most straightforward - it’s depicting a political and social landscape of the past as a hellish nightmare, while drawing parallels to the present of the country. The fact that he soon after fled the country for many years, only to return and have his funding canceled during filming proves his perspective correct in many ways.

As for the film itself, it has some clearly symbolic figures that are strikingly unnatural, which to me matches the surreal tone and makes the allegory stand out. There’s no question whether the nun figure is supposed to represent a “real person living a normal life”. This makes the scenes, like the bewildering cut to the two emerging from a burning, delapidated house resonate more strongly for the allegorical context as well. It’s also just an amazing shot. As is the nun stripping her hood and seemingly abandoning her faith. These are images that don’t need words to explain, they convey feeling powerfully, even if disorientatingly.

I don’t understand all of the contextual references, not living in the time or the place that the film was written, but I don’t think this lack of specific knowledge hampers the comprehension of the fundamental thematic concepts. There is a literal devil running around slicing people’s throats, after all, it’s a timeless concept not uniquely relevant to communist Poland. Compared to other surrealist films, the handheld camerawork feels far more immediate to me, which to me makes it all the more compelling as a visceral experience as opposed to merely an interpretative experience.

You can talk about the backstabbing and prostitution and invest and murder as political commentary, and it’s interesting in that aspect, but as a surrealist hellscape it’s rivaled by very few films. One of them is his later On the Silver Globe, which operates in a similarly satirical, polemicist tone, and both are incredible and unique.
